one_line_takeaway: Fed-Listing shows that a passive, honest-but-curious FedGNN server can reconstruct a target client's private label distribution with high fidelity from final-layer gradients alone, and that standard defenses (DP, noisy gradients, gradient compression) only meaningfully blunt the attack once they are strong enough to substantially degrade model utility.
key_finding: On GCN, Fed-Listing (using real auxiliary data) generally achieves the lowest MD and JS-divergence and the highest CS versus random guessing and Decaf across datasets and scenarios, e.g., near-perfect performance on Citeseer equal-proportion (JS=0.000, CS=0.973), and CS=0.971/JS=0.002 on Cora one-class-missing versus Decaf's CS=0.762. Performance narrows in extreme non-i.i.d. settings and Decaf occasionally overtakes on specific metrics (e.g., Citeseer single-class-only, where Fed-Listing CS=0.391 vs Decaf CS=0.469). The active-attacker baseline EC-LDA achieves near-perfect results (CS near 1.000, JS near 0.000) across the board, serving as an upper bound rather than a fair peer comparison. Robustness to defenses: differential privacy, noisy gradients, and gradient compression were tested on GCN/Cora; noisy-gradient defense caused model accuracy to fall from 0.701 (sigma=0.5) to 0.473 (sigma=3) while still not eliminating attack success at moderate noise; gradient compression improved accuracy from 0.547 (alpha=0.1) to 0.725 (alpha=1.0) with attack JS-divergence minimized (0.118) around alpha=0.5. The paper concludes all three defenses only meaningfully suppress the attack when pushed to a strength that substantially degrades model utility. Ablations: last-layer gradients give up to 479x training-time speedup over all-layer gradients (parameter dimension 4,800 vs 2,962,400 on Amazon Computers) with comparable or better attack accuracy in most settings; MLP attack model (38,919 parameters) achieves comparable CS (0.985) to a CNN attack model (1,740,583 parameters, CS=0.980) with far fewer parameters and faster prediction time.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Fed-Listing, a passive gradient-based Label Distribution Inference (LDI) attack targeting horizontal Federated Graph Neural Networks (FedGNNs). An honest-but-curious FL server exploits only the final-layer gradients that clients routinely exchange during training, combined with an auxiliary dataset, to reconstruct the per-class label proportions of a target client's private training data without accessing raw node features or the adjacency matrix. The method works by (1) partitioning an auxiliary dataset into shadow-FL instances under four synthetic client distribution strategies (equal proportion, random/non-i.i.d., single-class, missing-class), (2) running shadow federated training to record last-layer gradient trajectories paired with their known ground-truth label distributions, and (3) training a neural-network attack model (MLP, 256/128 hidden units) with a composite L1 + Jensen-Shannon-divergence loss (L = alpha*L_L1 + (1-alpha)*L_JS) to map a client's gradient signature to its label distribution.
threat_model: Honest-but-curious FL server as adversary. Adversary knowledge: full access to model parameters/gradients shared by clients each round, knowledge of the global label space, and access to an auxiliary dataset with a distribution and label space similar to the real client data. Adversary capability: the server passively observes last-layer gradients across communication rounds and does not tamper with training (contrasted explicitly against the concurrent active-attacker baseline EC-LDA, which manipulates/clips global parameters before broadcasting them and is described as more detectable). This is a passive, stealthy attack setting specific to horizontal FedGNNs (as opposed to prior LDI work on image data or vertical FL).
limitations: The attack requires an auxiliary dataset with a similar distribution and identical label space to the target client's data -- a nontrivial assumption relaxed only partially via synthetic data (GraphUniverse), which the authors note performs worse than real auxiliary data 'likely because feature vectors and edge densities are generated randomly without any optimization.' Comparisons to EC-LDA are explicitly not apples-to-apples since it is an active attacker with a stronger threat model; the paper itself frames EC-LDA results as a reference/upper bound, not a peer baseline. Evaluation for GraphSage and GIN architectures (beyond GCN) is deferred to supplementary material not included in the main PDF text reviewed here. The single-class-only and highly skewed scenarios show performance gaps narrowing or reversing versus Decaf on some metrics/datasets (e.g., Citeseer, PubMed single-class-only), indicating the attack is not uniformly dominant. No real-world (non-benchmark) deployment or human-subject validation of the inferred label distributions' sensitivity is presented.

datasets_used: Amazon Computers (13,752 nodes, 245,861 edges, 767 features, 10 classes)
Synthetic auxiliary graphs generated via GraphUniverse
quantitative_results: Citeseer, equal proportion, GCN: Fed-Listing JS=0.000, CS=0.973 (real auxiliary data)
Cora, one-class missing, GCN: Fed-Listing CS=0.971, JS=0.002
PubMed, one-class dominant, synthetic auxiliary: MD=0.546, JS=0.017, CS=0.861 (best synthetic-auxiliary result)
Noisy-gradient defense: model accuracy drops from 0.701 (sigma=0.5) to 0.473 (sigma=3.0)
Gradient compression defense: model accuracy rises from 0.547 (alpha=0.1) to 0.725 (alpha=1.0); attack JS-divergence minimized at 0.118 near alpha=0.5
Last-layer vs all-layer gradients on Amazon Computers: up to 479x training-time speedup (parameter dim 4,800 vs 2,962,400)
MLP attack model: CS=0.985, 38,919 parameters, ~0.000s prediction time; CNN attack model: CS=0.980, 1,740,583 parameters, 0.014s prediction time (random-split scenario, GCN target)
baselines_compared: Random guessing (samples label proportions from a normal distribution)
Decaf (Dai et al., IEEE TIFS 2024) -- LDI attack originally for image-domain FL, adapted to graph input as a passive baseline

EC-LDA (Cheng et al., 2025, concurrent work) -- active-attacker LDI attack against federated graph learning via embedding compression, used as an upper-bound reference rather than a directly comparable passive baseline
